coredata.dc:title: Fluorescence-based detection of thiamethoxam in agricultural and fruit samples by green synthesized N-CQDs from Citrus sinensis
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of Food Composition and Analysis
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 Elsevier Inc. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier Inc. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Rishabh,
Rani, Manviri
Shanker, Uma
coredata.dc:description: Neonicotinoid insecticides, such as thiamethoxam (TMX), are widely used worldwide in agricultural practices. Environmental hazards associated with the longevity and toxicity of TMX and analogous pesticides include its harmful effects on pollinators, soil integrity, and aquatic systems. This study presents an environmentally sustainable synthesis method for nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots (N-CQDs) derived from Citrus sinensis peels, aimed at detecting TMX in environmental matrices such as agricultural runoff prevalent in pesticide-intensive farming regions. This biosensor utilizes the pronounced fluorescence characteristics of N-CQDs to detect TMX contamination. Consequently, we note the influence of static quenching resulting from hydrogen bonding and π-π interactions in the current system, with a detection limit of 2.36 × 10⁻⁶ molL−1 or 0.6844 ppm. The recoveries from the spiked fruit samples and agricultural runoff are validated within the range of 96.50 %-104.30 %, demonstrating the efficacy of this sensor for monitoring TMX residues in food and water, hence contributing to environmental and human health protection.
coredata.dcterms:subject: N-doped CQDs
Green synthesis
Thiamethoxam
Fluorescence-Based Detection

Examining selectivity and quenching of fluorescence
The interaction of TMX with N-CQDs
Estimation of TMX recovery in spiked samples

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: Dr Manviri Rani and Dr Uma Shanker are grateful to DST-SERB, New Delhi, India (Sanction order no. CRG/2023/002740 & EEQ/2023/000196) for funding this project. Mr. Rishabh is thankful to the Ministry of Education (MoE) in New Delhi for a research fellowship. Characterization support from Material Research Centre MNIT Jaipur and IIC, NIT Jalandhar, India, is greatly acknowledged.

Dr MR and Dr US are grateful to DST-SERB, New Delhi (Sanction order no. CRG/2023/002740 & EEQ/2023/000196) for funding this project. Ms. SS is thankful to the Ministry of Education (MoE) in New Delhi for a research fellowship. Characterization support from Material Research Centre MNIT Jaipur and IIC, NIT Jalandhar, India, is greatly acknowledged.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic representation for synthesis of N-CQDs from Citrus sinensis peels.
(a) HR-TEM image, (b) EDS, (c) Particle size distribution histogram of N-CQDs.
XPS spectra of N-CQDs (a) Full XPS spectrum. (b-d). Deconvoluted N1s, C1s, and O1s spectra.
(a) Powder X-Ray Diffraction (PXRD) spectra, (b) Fourier-transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR) spectra of N-CQDs.
(a) Fluorescence emission spectra of N-CQDs at different excitation wavelengths ranging from 190 to 260 nm (b) Absorbance spectrum of N-CQDs (inset: blue emission under UV lamp.
N-CQDs stability studies: (a) number of days (b) effect of pH on PL intensity, (c) ionic strength of KCl and (d) different time periods.

(a) Fluorescence intensity ratio of the N-CQDs solutions containing different pesticides, (b) Fluorescence spectra in the presence of different concentrations of TMX (1–50 ppm), (c) Calibration graph for the determination of the TMX.
(a) N-CQDs and N-CQDs + TMX FTIR spectra. (b) Absorbance spectra of N-CQDs with TMX. (c) Fluorescence decay curves of N-CQDs and N-CQDs + TMX, (d) Schematic representation of TMX sensing by N-CQDs.
Determination of TMX in Various Samples Using N-CQDs as a Fluorometric Probe (n = 3).
Comparison of Different Methods for Detecting Thiamethoxam.

head.ce:title: Fluorescence-based detection of thiamethoxam in agricultural and fruit samples by green synthesized N-CQDs from Citrus sinensis

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Neonicotinoid insecticides, such as thiamethoxam (TMX), are widely used worldwide in agricultural practices. Environmental hazards associated with the longevity and toxicity of TMX and analogous pesticides include its harmful effects on pollinators, soil integrity, and aquatic systems. This study presents an environmentally sustainable synthesis method for nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots (N-CQDs) derived from Citrus sinensis peels, aimed at detecting TMX in environmental matrices such as agricultural runoff prevalent in pesticide-intensive farming regions. This biosensor utilizes the pronounced fluorescence characteristics of N-CQDs to detect TMX contamination. Consequently, we note the influence of static quenching resulting from hydrogen bonding and π-π interactions in the current system, with a detection limit of 2.36 × 10⁻⁶ molL−1 or 0.6844 ppm. The recoveries from the spiked fruit samples and agricultural runoff are validated within the range of 96.50 %-104.30 %, demonstrating the efficacy of this sensor for monitoring TMX residues in food and water, hence contributing to environmental and human health protection.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Neonicotinoid pesticides, such as thiamethoxam (TMX), are increasingly used in various farming regions, mainly around major agricultural centers. These agents have been proven to efficiently control a wide range of pests (Simon-Delso et al., 2015). In the local context, TMX is applied to crops like maize and cotton plus orchard fruits, which increases the yields of the crops (Wu et al.,2021; Abdulhussein et al., 2021; Han et al., 2022). Over the past few years, its persistence in soil and water has become the most critical environmental issue, mainly where runoff from agricultural activities contaminates water sources, adversely affecting local ecosystems and posing health risks to communities nearby (Shi et al., 2023; Luo et al., 2021; Yang et al., 2022). Although it is highly used in agriculture, TMX has been linked to severe health risks, including liver carcinogenesis, nephrotoxicity, and reproductive toxicity (Zhu et al., 2019; Thompson et al., 2020; Kong et al., 2022). In 2018, the European Union banned TMX from being sprayed outdoors since it causes harm by killing pollinators and other bees and affecting the general environment (EFSA, 2018). Different countries have also raised MRLs of TMX for various kinds of food products, including honey and milk, up to a maximum limit of 0.05 mg/kg (EFSA, 2021). Such regulatory actions indicate the need for susceptible, reliable, and accessible methods to detect TMX residues in environmental and food matrices.

Most conventional methods of pesticide detection rely on techniques such as high-performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) and liquid chromatography-mass spectrometry (LC-MS). They are often associated with high accuracy but are cost-intensive and complex, requiring preparation of samples for a considerable amount of time (Peng et al., 2021; Jiang et al., 2018; Shi et al., 2023; Luo et al., 2021; Yang et al., 2022). Electrochemical sensors and enzyme-linked immunosorbent assays (ELISA) also constitute widely used detectors, but limitations in sensitivity and selectivity under different conditions restrain their applications. Thus, fluorescence-based sensors have been recognized as sensitive, fast-rate-of-detection, and easy-to-use optical sensors for the monitoring of pesticides in various settings (Gao et al., 2017; Kumar & Kim, 2022; He and Du., 2023; Ma et al., 2024). Recently, carbon-based nanomaterials, particularly nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots (N-CQDs), have been highlighted as a promising technology for TMX detection. Nitrogen is the most frequently occurring element that serves as a charge carrier. As such, nitrogen alters the properties of N-CQDs. The proximity in atomic radius of carbon and nitrogen and five valence electrons in the outer shell of the latter allow the potential for highly stable bonding between carbon and nitrogen (Liang et al., 2018). Nitrogen complements a donor atom as a feature of the charge carrier of the n-type atom, therefore improving the performance of N-CQDs in most detection applications (Soldano et al., 2013; Mei et al., 2024; Mansour et al., 2024). Due to its position on the surface of CQDs as an n-type, it aids in the electron transit during LUMO excitation; this promotes self-fluorescence quenching and high analytical application. There are also varying methods of carbon quantum dot synthesis that have been reported using methods that do not use chemicals and are based on readily available natural resources (Rasal et al., 2021). Although fennel seeds and lemon juice have been used as precursors for the synthesis of carbon quantum dots in chemical synthesis, relatively fewer approaches utilize the precursor feedstock from biomass waste (Wang et al., 2020a,2020b; Wang et al., 2020a,2020b; Rasal et al., 2021).

This work synthesized N-CQDs using a green synthesis method from Citrus sinensis peels. The obtained N-CQDs showed excellent fluorescence properties, making them very efficient for detecting TMX residues in agricultural runoff and fruit samples. Our work shall bridge laboratory-scale nanotechnology research with practical application contexts in real-world settings as an approach towards TMX residue monitoring in agro-environmental settings. The work would benefit food safety and the environment if the developed technologies also help overcome the limitations of conventional pesticide detection techniques.
Keywords: Nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots (N-CQDs), Thiamethoxam (TMX), Pesticide detection, Fluorescence quenching, Environmental monitoring.

Fresh Citrus sinensis peels were collected directly from local Fruit seller. Pesticides, such as Atrazine (AT), P-Cresol (PC), Thiamethoxam (TMX), Fipronil (FPN), Indoxcarb (IDC), and Chlorpyrifos (CP), were procured from Sigma Aldrich, USA. Deionized water was used as the solvent for all the experimental methods. Methanol with a minimum purity of 99.9 %, acetonitrile (ACN) with a minimum purity of 99.9 %, and dichloromethane (DCM) with a minimum purity of 99.9 % were obtained from Sigma Aldrich. These chemicals are of HPLC quality.

Citrus sinensis peels, initially cleaned and oven-dried at 50 °C, were pulverized into a fine powder. 5 g powdered peels were mixed with 40 distilled water in a beaker. Concurrently, a urea solution (2 g urea in 10 mL water) was prepared and introduced into the beaker. The mixture underwent microwave irradiation (800 W) for 12 minutes. Subsequently, 20 distilled water was incorporated, and the resulting solution was subjected to centrifugation, filtration, and dialysis against distilled water for 48 hours. The resultant N-CQDs solution was stored at 4 °C in the refrigerator ( Fig. 1).

To explore N-CQDs' selectivity towards pesticides based on their FL behaviour, a mixture combining 50-ppm (1 mL) pesticide solution was prepared with N-CQDs solution (25 μL). FL intensities were then measured after a 15-minute equilibration period. The experiment was repeated using varying pesticide concentrations (TMX) ranging from 1 to 50 ppm with N-CQDs under the same conditions. Additionally, a line of testing was conducted using N-CQDs with TMX (5, 10, and 15 ppm) in agriculture runoff water samples to assess their effectiveness in real-world scenarios.

The synthesized N-CQDs were tested using spiked samples of fruits (Citrus sinensis, grapes, apples, and guava). These fresh fruits were obtained from the NIT Jalandhar campus and processed without extra treatment. They were initially crushed and homogenized using a household mixer grinder, followed by Whatman filter paper filtration to remove larger fragments. The filtered samples were then diluted tenfold with deionized water. Subsequently, the diluted samples were spiked with various concentrations of TMX (5, 10, and 15 ppm) and allowed to stand for 20 min. Afterward, methanol (10 mL) was added, and the mixture was vortexed for 10 minutes to ensure uniform distribution. Then, 3 g of sodium sulfate was introduced to facilitate dehydration, and the samples were filtered through a 0.45 μm syringe filter. Lastly, the solvent was evaporated to concentrate the solution, and the concentrations of TMX were determined using fluorescence spectroscopy.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Calculation for quantum yield QY measurements
ce:section.ce:para: Quantum yield (QY) is generally stated as the number of emitted photons depending on the absorbance photon number. When calculating the QY of a material, its value should be compared with the known quantum yield (Başkaya SK et al., 2022). Quinine sulfate aqueous solution obtained by dissolving in 0.01 M H2SO4 was utilized as a standard (QY = 0.54 at 340 nm) to calculate the QY of the obtained N-CQDs from Citrus sinensis.

The formula calculated QY of as-prepared N-CQDs:
(1)
Q
CD = Q
s
(A
s ∕A
CD
) (I
CD ∕I
S
) (n
CD ∕n
s
)
2
In the formula, QY gives the quantum yield, A the absorbance, I the emission area, and n the diffraction index. The subscript CD and S represent carbon dots and standard, respectively.

The N-CQDs have a roughly spherical morphology and are uniformly distributed, according to the TEM picture ( Fig. 2a). These N-CQDs have a relatively limited size distribution with an average diameter of roughly 6.34 nm (Fig. 2c). The size of this tiny object aligns with the anticipated measurements for quantum dots, which are recognized to display quantum confinement characteristics. The successful doping of nitrogen into the carbon lattice is confirmed by the HRTEM image and with XRD analysis, which also shows that the N-CQDs have a semi-crystalline structure with visible lattice fringes that match the (002) planes of graphitic carbon [Rani and Shanker, 2024]. The EDS spectra of N-CQDs confirmed the presence of carbon (C), nitrogen (N), and oxygen (O) molecules. The quantitative test revealed the following elemental composition: carbon (72.3 wt%), nitrogen (16.5 wt%), and oxygen (11.2 wt%). A noticeable nitrogen peak indicates that nitrogen has been successfully doped into the carbon matrix, which is required to modify the electrical properties of the carbon dots (Fig. 2b).

XPS examination of synthesized N-CQDs reveals their surface composition and bonding states. Three prominent peaks of N 1 s, C 1 s, and O 1 s in the survey spectrum ( Fig. 3a) have identified carbon, nitrogen, and oxygen in this composite. Quantum dots are carbonaceous, and the dominant peak of C 1 s at 284–287 eV signifies carbon as the significant element. Peaks at 530 eV and 400 eV account for O 1 s and N 1 s and, respectively, indicate the presence of nitrogen doping and oxygen-containing functional groups on N-CQDs. Nitrogen doping changes the electronic structure and improves quantum dots' optical and catalytic capabilities, while oxygen groups are likely to increase hydrophilicity and surface reactivity. Deconvolution of the high-resolution N 1 s spectrum (Fig. 3b) gives two main peaks. Pyridinic nitrogen peaks at 399.7 eV, while graphitic nitrogen peaks at 401 eV (Cano et al., 2019). Pyridinic nitrogen offers electrons to the carbon framework and thus enhances catalytic and conductive properties, while graphitic nitrogen enhances the photoluminescence and stability of N-CQDs. This will evidence the presence of such nitrogen species, indicating the successful integration of nitrogen into the carbon structure and thus enhancing the potential of the N-CQDs for catalysis, bioimaging, and sensing applications (Cano et al., 2019).

Peaks in the deconstructed C1s spectrum (Fig. 3c) are due to carbon bonding states. Sp2-hybridized carbon (CC), graphitic carbon backbone is at 284.6 eV, while C-N and CO bonding maxima are at 285.7 and 287.2 eV (Blume et al., 2015). These functional groups suggest that nitrogen and oxygen are bonded to the carbon skeleton, influencing the surface chemistry and allowing for higher solubility and stability of quantum dots in aqueous solutions (Kotronia et al., 2021). A C-O or CO group shows that partial carbon surface oxidation occurred during the synthesis process, which can potentially increase N-CQD photoluminescence and biocompatibility (Crispi et al., 2023).

In conclusion, the O 1 s spectra of Fig. 3d are identical to those observed with a single peak at 532 eV; this implies oxygen-containing functional groups such as CO and C-O bonds (Castorina et al., 2019) were detected. These functional groups enhance N-CQDs' hydrophilicity, making them suitable for biological and aqueous applications. The XPS studies have revealed that N-CQDs are carbon-rich cores with nitrogen and oxygen doping, improving electrical, optical, and surface properties to make them suitable for many applications (Huang et al., 2019).

The XRD pattern of the prepared N-CQDs in Fig. 4a shows one prominent peak at about 23°, corresponding to the graphitic carbon plane (002). A reflection so broad indicates that the N-CQDs have an amorphous or semi-crystalline structure. Such behavior is typical for carbon-based quantum dots due to their small size and defects or functional groups on their surface. The broadness of the peak from the material indicated that it would be lacking in long-range order, characteristic of graphitic or SP2-hybridized carbon atoms that are arrayed in a somewhat disordered structure and consistent with previous findings on carbon-based nanomaterials (Li et al., 2021; Verma et al., 2021).

The FT-IR spectrum of N-CQDs (Fig. 4b) shows the surface functional groups of nanoparticles. Absorption bands at 3200–3500 cm⁻¹ were assigned to be caused by stretching vibrations of N-H and O-H groups, which indicates that N-CQDs contain hydroxyl groups and amine groups on their surface, facilitating their hydrophilicity along with potential functionalization. This is evidenced by a strong peak at 1630 cm⁻¹ of CO stretching, thus establishing carbonyl groups' existence. The other peaks result at 1400–1500 cm⁻¹ and 1100 cm⁻¹ arising from C-H/C-N and C-O stretching vibration, respectively, showing aliphatic as well as nitrogen functionality (Pomal et al., 2021; Liu et al., 2021). These surface functional groups are significant in controlling N-CQDs' optical properties and stability, enhancing their utility in bioimaging, sensing, and catalysis (John et al., 2021).

Fig. 5a displays the FL emission spectra of N-CQDs when excited at wavelengths between 190 and 260 nm. Its diverse functional groups can cause wavelength-dependent variations in peak intensities for FL emissions of N-CQD. These are traps for the excitation energy, which induces defects (Liang et al., 2022; Jin et al., 2019). The differences in FL emission are attributed to surface functionalities of N-CQDs, including functional group types. Other essential factors include changes on the surface, particle size, and heteroatoms (Zhao et al., 2018; Tian and Yin., 2019; Singh et al., 2020). The highest emission spectra intensity is attained at 776 nm when excited at 240 nm. The UV–vis absorption spectra of N-CQDs are displayed in Fig. 5b. Polycyclic aromatic structures' absorption is indicated as a peak at 214 nm. The inset highlights the strong blue fluorescence of the synthesized N-CQDs under UV light (365 nm excitation). Further investigation of fluorescence properties was conducted to find the quantum yield calculated to be 38.48 % by using quinine sulfate as the reference at 54 % (Figure S1) (Eaton., 1988; Würth et al., 2013).

The long-term stability of the N-CQDs was determined by measuring emission intensity at 240 nm after 50-day storage at 5°C ( Fig. 6a). The obtained result lies in the limits of less than 0.9 % fluctuation, which is stable over time. Thus, N-CQDs are promising for practical sensing applications and commercial use. Properties pH-dependent were investigated by dilution: 25 μL of N-CQDs was diluted in DI water (5 mL), and then the solution was whirled for 120 seconds. A pH meter registered The FL emission spectrum over the pH range of 3–10. Fig. 6b treats the data with buffer solutions, leading to pH 3 and 7. From the study, the acidic pH range of 3–6 and the basic pH range of 8–10 indicated that the maximum intensity of FL was at pH 7. Consequently, the intensity of FL decreased with a variation in acidity and basicity values. Both protonation and deprotonation characteristics of N-CQDs are indirectly affected by the existence of H+ or OH- ions interacting with the functional groups of these particles and causing such changes.

Moreover, the concentration of the KCl solution increases from 0 to 2 M to verify the impact of ionic strength on the fluorescence properties of N-CQDs. The results showed no significant changes in the intensities of FL across this range (Fig. 6c), demonstrating that ionic strength exerts no influence on N-CQD fluorescence. Additionally, photostability studies under UV light at 365 nm for 30 min in Fig. 6d revealed that the FL intensity of N-CQDs remained amazingly stable, indicating robust photostability. In summary, our results greatly emphasize the long-lasting photoluminescence properties of N-CQDs across a wide range of conditions, such as varying pH levels, long storage times, and altering ionic strengths. Stability has established the flexibility of N-CQDs in sensing applications.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Examining selectivity and quenching of fluorescence

ce:section.ce:para: The incubation time and pH had been optimized to strengthen the applicability of the fluorescence quenching method, as depicted in Figure. The obtained outcomes revealed that pH and the incubation time did not significantly affect the FL quenching efficiency of the sensor system (F0/F). The outcomes of the experiments were carried out at a pH of 7 and an incubation time of 10 minutes. Attempts were made to achieve fluorescence detection of pesticides by equilibrating N-CQDs with different solutions containing pesticides at 50 ppm. This study compares N-CQDs' selectivity toward various pesticides at an excitation wavelength of 240 nm. Fig. 7a of the present study shows that the FL intensity and quenching levels were affected by different pesticides, including atrazine (AT), cresol (PC), thiamethoxam (TMX), fipronil (FPN), indoxcarb (IDC), and chlorpyrifos (CP).

Under the specified conditions, different concentrations of TMX (ranging from 1 to 50 ppm) were added to the N-CQD solution. A linear relationship between the fluorescence quenching ratio (F0/F) and TMX concentration was established, as illustrated in Fig. 7b. The regression equation, with a (Ksv) slope of 0.0174 and (R2) correlation coefficient of 0.99749, is shown in Fig. 7c. With the following equation, the limit of detection (LOD) for TMX was determined to be 0.6844 ppm (2.36 × 10⁻⁶ mol/L):
(2) LOD = 3σ/m
In this case, σ represents the standard deviation of the regression lines' y-intercepts, and m is the calibration graph's slope.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The interaction of TMX with N-CQDs
ce:section.ce:para: The interaction between TMX and N-CQDs was thoroughly analyzed through FTIR, UV–vis spectra, and fluorescence lifetime data. FTIR spectra reveal significant changes upon adding TMX, including a shift and reduction in the intensity of the broad peak around 3400 cm⁻¹ , attributed to -OH and -NH groups. This shift suggests hydrogen bonding with TMX molecules. Changes in the peaks associated with CO and CC stretching vibrations around 1650 cm⁻¹ also point to possible interactions such as π-π stacking or electrostatic forces ( Fig. 8a). UV–vis spectroscopy complements these findings, showing notable quenching of a specific absorbance peak of N-CQDs upon TMX addition. The absence of new peaks post-interaction implies that TMX's structural integrity remains unchanged and that the interactions primarily involve N-CQDs' surface functionalities (Fig. 8b).

Fluorescence lifetime measurements, obtained through Time-Correlated Single Photon Counting (TCSPC), reveal that the fluorescence lifetime of N-CQDs remains nearly constant at 4.87 ns and 4.56 ns before and after TMX addition, respectively (Table S1). Fig. 8c shows the fluorescence decay curves of N-CQDs and N-CQDs + TMX. This lack of significant change suggests that the quenching mechanism is static rather than dynamic. In static quenching, the presence of TMX leads to forming a non-fluorescent ground-state complex with N-CQDs, which reduces fluorescence without altering the excited state dynamics. The proposed mechanism, illustrated in Fig. 8d, involves hydrogen bonding between the amine and hydroxyl groups of N-CQDs and the nitro and cyano groups of TMX. This interaction supports a static quenching mechanism where TMX effectively reduces N-CQDs' fluorescence through ground-state complex formation rather than changes in excited state lifetimes. Overall, this comprehensive analysis underscores the potential of N-CQDs as sensitive materials for detecting TMX, with their interaction leading to measurable changes in FTIR, UV–vis spectra, and fluorescence properties.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Estimation of TMX recovery in spiked samples
ce:section.ce:para: After evaluating N-CQDs' selectivity in detecting TMX, their practical application was assessed for analyzing these compounds in spiked fruits (Citrus sinensis, grapes, apple, and Guava) and agriculture runoff samples commonly found to contain pesticides. The fruit samples obtained from a local marketplace in NIT Jalandhar, Punjab, India, were prepared separately using the aforementioned method. Both fruits and agriculture runoff samples were then treated with varying concentrations of TMX (5, 10, and 15 μM), and the level of this pesticide was determined using the described procedure. The recovery rates of TMX varied from 96.50 % to 104.30 %, with RSDs ranging from 5.08 % to 16.50 % ( Table 1). These findings indicate that the N-CQDs-fluorescence method is suitable for detecting TMX in actual samples.

This study's N-CQD sensor for neonicotinoid pesticide detection, particularly TMX, is competitive and environmentally friendly. The current research outperforms the literature in many ways. The N-CQDs sensor has a linearity range of 3.43–171.4 × 10⁻⁶ molL−1, comparable to or greater than other methods like β-cyclodextrin and graphene-modified electrochemical sensors (5 − 160 × 10⁻⁶ molL−1) and molecularly imprinted graphene sensors (5 − 200 × 10⁻⁶ molL−1), but narrower than Co₃O nanoparticle-based sensors (Zhai et al., 2017; Xie et al., 2017). The current linearity range is sufficient to track environmentally necessary TMX amounts.

Our N-CQDs sensor has a high LOD value of 2.36 × 10⁻⁶ molL−1, surpassing several carbon-based sensors. Poly(l-ornithine) modified glassy carbon electrodes have a higher LOD of 1.7 × 10⁻⁸ molL−1 (Zhang et al., 2022), while β-cyclodextrin-graphene composite sensors have a LOD of 2.7 × 10⁻⁷ molL−1 (Zhai et al., 2017). The electrochemical sensor using Co₃O₄ nanoparticles has a higher sensitive LOD (4.9 × 10⁻¹⁰ molL−1) (Ganesamurthi et al., 2020). This technology lacks the simplicity and eco-friendliness of the N-CQDs method. Although fluorescence-based technologies such as the Zr(Ⅳ) metal-organic framework platform (Xu et al., 2022) and fluorescent aptasensors (Luo et al., 2021) have lower LOD values, their laborious preparation and materials limit their practical use. Additionally, Table 2 compares our approach's LOD with previously published CD-based sensors, further highlighting the effectiveness of the N-CQDs sensor (Kong et al., 2022; Luo et al., 2021; Shi et al., 2023).

Finally, the green synthesis of N-CQDs using Citrus sinensis peels uses renewable waste material, making it environmentally and economically beneficial. Our sustainable technology is better for large-scale, eco-friendly applications than other sensors, which use harmful chemicals or expensive nanomaterials. The N-CQDs sensor has also shown practical utility in agricultural runoff and spiked fruit samples, with recovery rates between 96.50 % and 104.30 %. The sensor is robust in complicated matrices and suitable for environmental and food safety monitoring in real life. In conclusion, the N-CQDs-based sensor has an equivalent detection limit and linearity range to established methods. Green synthesis and strong sample recovery rates are superior for practical, ecologically safe pesticide detection.

The synthesis of N-CQDs from Citrus sinensis peels offers a cost-effective, environmentally friendly solution for detecting TMX. The green synthesis method aligns with eco-friendly principles and produces N-CQDs with desirable optical and structural properties, including high fluorescence stability, selectivity, and sensitivity. Fluorescence quenching experiments demonstrated that N-CQDs interact with TMX through static quenching, primarily involving hydrogen bonding and π-π interactions. The developed sensor achieved a remarkable limit of detection of 2.36 × 10⁻⁶ molL−1 (0.6844 ppm), surpassing previously reported carbon-dot-based methods. Furthermore, real-world applications, such as TMX detection in spiked fruit and agricultural runoff samples, confirmed the practical viability of this sensor, with recovery rates exceeding 96 %. These findings underscore N-CQDs' potential for environmental monitoring and food safety, offering a sustainable and efficient approach to pesticide residue detection. This work contributes to the advancement of green nanotechnology in environmental sensing applications, paving the way for broader use of N-CQDs in monitoring harmful chemicals in the ecosystem.

Manviri Rani: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Data curation, Conceptualization. Uma Shanker: Writing – review & editing, Visualization, Supervision, Software, Conceptualization. Rishabh: Writing – original draft, Software, Methodology, Formal analysis, Data curation.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: Dr Manviri Rani and Dr Uma Shanker are grateful to DST-SERB, New Delhi, India (Sanction order no. CRG/2023/002740 & EEQ/2023/000196) for funding this project. Mr. Rishabh is thankful to the Ministry of Education (MoE) in New Delhi for a research fellowship. Characterization support from Material Research Centre MNIT Jaipur and IIC, NIT Jalandhar, India, is greatly acknowledged.

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.jfca.2024.107109.
